event: Jehu's companions finding the remains of Jezebel
symbols: men discovering scattered remains — the fulfillment of Elijah's prophecy against Jezebel
dogs in the scene — the dogs that devoured Jezebel's flesh as prophesied
massive city wall — the wall of Jezreel from which Jezebel was thrown down
darkness and shadow — the grim nature of divine judgment against idolatry and murder
description: A detailed engraving showing several bearded men in robes standing near the base of a massive stone wall or fortification. They look down at something on the ground with expressions of shock and horror. A small dog is visible near the base of the wall. The composition is dominated by the towering dark wall taking up most of the right side. Below the image is an engraved title identifying the scene. The technique shows fine cross-hatching creating dramatic contrasts between the dark wall and the lit figures.
theological_description: The discovery of Jezebel's remains is one of the most vivid fulfillments of prophetic judgment in the Old Testament. Years earlier, Elijah had prophesied after Jezebel orchestrated the murder of Naboth for his vineyard: 'In the territory of Jezreel the dogs shall eat the flesh of Jezebel, and the corpse of Jezebel shall be as dung on the face of the field' (2KGS.9.36-37). When Jehu's men went to bury her — for she was 'a king's daughter' — they found nothing but skull, feet, and palms of hands (2KGS.9.35). The precision of the prophecy's fulfillment demonstrates the biblical principle that divine justice, though it may delay, never fails. Jezebel represents the archetype of idolatrous royal power — she had introduced Baal worship into Israel, murdered the prophets of the Lord, and corrupted the judicial system to seize Naboth's property. Her end serves as a warning that no earthly power can ultimately withstand the purposes of God.

scripture_refs.reason: The death of Jezebel — thrown from the window, trampled, and consumed by dogs
Jehu's men finding only skull, feet, and palms — the scene depicted in the engraving
Elijah's original prophecy that dogs would devour Jezebel by the wall of Jezreel
The condemnation of the woman Jezebel in the letter to Thyatira — Jezebel as an archetype of idolatrous corruption
